coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of dodecylamine-functionalized graphene quantum dots and their application as stabilizers in an emulsion polymerization of styrene
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Xuan, Wang
Ruiyi, Li
Zaijun, Li
Junkang, Liu
coredata.dc:description: Pickering emulsions have attracted considerable interest due to their potential applications in many fields, such as the food, pharmaceutical, petroleum and cosmetics industries. The study reports the synthesis of dodecylamine-functionalized graphene quantum dots (d-GQDs) and their implementation as stabilizers in an emulsion polymerization of styrene. First, d-GQDs are prepared by thermal pyrolysis of citric acid and dodecylamine in 0.1M ammonium hydroxide. The resulting d-GQDs consist of small graphene sheets with abundant amino, carboxyl, acylamino, hydroxyl and alkyl chains on the edge. The amphiphilic structure gives the d-GQDs high surface activity. The addition of d-GQDs can reduce the surface tension of water to 30.8mNm−1 and the interfacial tension of paraffin oil/water to 0.0182mNm−1. The surface activity is much better than that of previously reported solid particle surfactants for Pickering emulsions and is close to that of sodium dodecylbenzenesulfonate, which is, a classical organic surfactants. Then, d-GQDs are employed as solid particle surfactants for stabilizing styrene-in-water emulsions. The emulsions exhibit excellent stability at pH 7. However, stability is lost when the pH is more than 9 or less than 4. The pH-switchable behaviour can be attributed to the protonation of amino groups in a weak acid medium and dissociation of carboxyl groups in a weak base medium. Finally, 2,2′-azobis(2-methylpropionitrile) is introduced into the Pickering emulsions to trigger emulsion polymerization of styrene. The as-prepared polystyrene spheres display a uniform morphology with a narrow diameter distribution. The fluorescent d-GQDs coated their surfaces. This study presents an approach for the fabrication of amphiphilic GQDs and GQDs-based functional materials, which have a wide range of potential applications in emulsion polymerization, as well as in sensors, catalysts, and energy storage.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Synthesis
Amphiphilicity
Solid particle surfactant
Emulsion polymerization
Polystyrene spheres

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characteristic of the Pickering emulsions stabilized by d-GQDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors acknowledge the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21576115) and the Prospective Joint Research Project: Cooperative Innovation Fund (No. BY2015019-26).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The procedure for the synthesis of d-GQDs.
Fluorescence spectra and optical photographs (insert) of the d-GQDs obtained by using A/C values of 0.01 (a), 0.02 (b), 0.04 (c), 0.06 (d), 0.08 (e) and 0.1 (f).
TEM image (A), particle size distribution (B) and AFM images of d-GQDs (C and D).
IR spectrum (A) and C1s (B) N1s (C) and O1s (D) XPS spectra of d-GQDs.
A: Surface tensions of water containing different concentrations of d-GQDs. B: interfacial tensions of paraffin oil/water containing different concentrations of d-GQDs.

Minimum surface tension of water in the presence of B-GQDs (a), O-GQDs (b), d-GQDs (c), GO (d), graphene (e), GQDs prepared by the thermal hydrolysis of citric acid (f), Cu2O (g) and SDBS (i) and in the absence of a surfactant (h).
The surface tension of 0.02gml−1 d-GQDs aqueous solution at different pH.
The pH-switching behaviours of Pickering emulsions for eight times.
SEM (A) and EDS images for total elements (B), nitrogen element (C) and oxygen element (D) of d-GQDs/PS.
SEM images of d-GQDs/PS prepared using 7mg of AIBN and 10 (A), 15 (B), 30 (C) and 60mgml−1 (D) d-GQDs.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of dodecylamine-functionalized graphene quantum dots and their application as stabilizers in an emulsion polymerization of styrene

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Synthetic and Biological Colloids, Ministry of Education, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Synthetic and Biological Colloids

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical and Material Engineering, Jiangnan University, Wuxi 214122, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical and Material Engineering

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Chemical and Material Engineering, Jiangnan University, Wuxi 214122, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical and Material Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The paper reported synthesis of dodecylamine-functionalized graphene quantum dots via thermal pyrolysis of citric acid and dodecylamine in an ammonium hydroxide solution. The graphene quantum dots exhibit high surface activity. It has been successfully applied as a stabilizer in Pickering emulsions polymerization of styrene.

Pickering emulsions have attracted considerable interest due to their potential applications in many fields, such as the food, pharmaceutical, petroleum and cosmetics industries. The study reports the synthesis of dodecylamine-functionalized graphene quantum dots (d-GQDs) and their implementation as stabilizers in an emulsion polymerization of styrene. First, d-GQDs are prepared by thermal pyrolysis of citric acid and dodecylamine in 0.1M ammonium hydroxide. The resulting d-GQDs consist of small graphene sheets with abundant amino, carboxyl, acylamino, hydroxyl and alkyl chains on the edge. The amphiphilic structure gives the d-GQDs high surface activity. The addition of d-GQDs can reduce the surface tension of water to 30.8mNm−1 and the interfacial tension of paraffin oil/water to 0.0182mNm−1. The surface activity is much better than that of previously reported solid particle surfactants for Pickering emulsions and is close to that of sodium dodecylbenzenesulfonate, which is, a classical organic surfactants. Then, d-GQDs are employed as solid particle surfactants for stabilizing styrene-in-water emulsions. The emulsions exhibit excellent stability at pH 7. However, stability is lost when the pH is more than 9 or less than 4. The pH-switchable behaviour can be attributed to the protonation of amino groups in a weak acid medium and dissociation of carboxyl groups in a weak base medium. Finally, 2,2′-azobis(2-methylpropionitrile) is introduced into the Pickering emulsions to trigger emulsion polymerization of styrene. The as-prepared polystyrene spheres display a uniform morphology with a narrow diameter distribution. The fluorescent d-GQDs coated their surfaces. This study presents an approach for the fabrication of amphiphilic GQDs and GQDs-based functional materials, which have a wide range of potential applications in emulsion polymerization, as well as in sensors, catalysts, and energy storage.

ce:section.ce:para: Pickering emulsions are stabilized by solid particles [1,2]. The stabilization of emulsion droplets takes place through adsorption of solid particles at the surface of emulsion droplets [3]. The emulsion stability often depends on the oil phase polarity [4], pH and ionic strength [5], monomer type [6] and other factors [7]. To improve emulsion stability and introduce functional materials, many nanomaterials have been developed as solid particle surfactants, such as silica nanoparticles [8,9], titania nanoparticles [10], ZIF-8 [11], ovalbumin [12], clay nanoparticles [13,14], Fe3O4 nanoparticles [15] and soft s protein microgel particles [16]. Recently, Pickering emulsions have attracted high interests due to potential applications in many fields such as food [17], pharmaceutical [18], petroleum [19] and cosmetics [20]. However, current solid particle surfactants for Pickering emulsions lack ideal surface activity, which may lead to poor emulsion stability. The fabrication of amphiphilic solid particles with high surface activity fo Pickering emulsions has received significant attention.

At the present, graphene is known as the most researched material in academia [21]. The growing interests of graphene are predominantly attributed to its remarkable properties, which have been shown to reach the highest theoretical limit known for materials [22]. The facile natures in the material preparation process and high production scalability have propelled extensive research, thus leading to the expansion of its applications in supercapacitors [23], lithium ion batteries [24], sensors [25], photodetectors [26] and drug delivery [27,28]. In recent years, graphene and its derivatives have also been investigated as solid surfactants for stabilizing Pickering emulsions [29]. Graphene has extremely high hydrophobicity and weak hydrophilicity. The inappropriate amphiphilicity gives it low surface activity. Pickering emulsions stabilized by graphene often display poor emulsion stability. To improve the amphiphilicity, graphene is hybridized with water-soluble macromolecules [30]. Contrary to graphene, graphene oxide (GO) contains more hydroxyl and epoxide groups on the basal planes and carboxylic acid groups at the edges. This structural characteristic gives GO a more appropriate amphiphilicity compared to graphene [31,32]. As a solid surfactant, GO also has inherent deficiencies from the micron size of the graphene sheets. These large graphene sheets can easily aggregate on the fluid interface and form a rigid interfacial layer, thus leading to low surface activity. To resolve this problem, Huang et al. developed an approach for the preparation of GO sheets with a lateral dimension smaller than 100nm via chemical exfoliation of graphite nanofibers [33]. The investigation proved that GO sheets are surfactants with size-dependent amphiphilicity. Smaller GO sheets should be more hydrophilic due to their higher density of charges originating from the ionized carboxyl groups on their edges. Thus, the nano-GO can act as a better dispersing agent for insoluble materials in water compared to regular GO, creating a more stable colloidal dispersion. Nano-GO has been used as the stabilizer in mini-emulsion polymerization for the construction of a graphene-based composite [34]. Compared with nano-GO, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) consist of smaller graphene sheets with more hydrophilic groups [35–37]. The nano-size allows the graphene sheets to be neatly arranged on an oil-water interface and to exhibit high surface activity. The number of hydrophilic groups means that GQDs have better water-solubility compared with GO. More importantly, the unique structure of GQDs leaves us a vast design space to adjust the amphiphilicity. Cho et al. functionalized GQDs with hexylamine and investigated how the polymerization reaction was affected by the amount of amine grafted onto the GQD surfaces [38]. Zeng et al. modified GQDs with octadecylamine and studied how the GQDs concentration and the relative proportions of oil and water in the emulsions influenced the type of emulsion stabilized by the GQDs [39]. These studies showed that the introduction of alkyl chain on the edge of graphene sheets is an effective approach to improve the surface activity of GQDs.

In the study, we report a facile synthesis of dodecylamine-functionalized graphene quantum dots (d-GQDs) via the thermal pyrolysis of citric acid and dodecylamine. The as-prepared d-GQDs offer excellent surface activity due to the appropriate amphiphilic structure. It is then successfully applied as a stabilizer in the emulsion polymerization of styrene.

Citric acid, 2,2′-azobis(2-methylpropionitrile) (AIBN), dodecylamine, ammonium hydroxide solution and styrene were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Mainland, China). Britton-Robinson buffer solution (BR buffer, H3PO4-HAc-H3BO3, 0.04M) was prepared and its pH was adjusted using 0.2M sodium hydroxide (NaOH) aqueous solution. Other reagents were purchased from Shanghai Chemical Company (Shanghai, China). All chemicals used were of analytical grade or of the highest purity available unless otherwise stated and used as received without further purification. Ultrapure water (18.2MΩcm) purified from a Milli-Q purification system was used throughout the experiment.

d-GQDs were synthesized by one-step thermal pyrolysis of citric acid and dodecylamine in an ammonium hydroxide solution [40]. In a typical synthesis, citric acid (0.96g), dodecylamine (0.056g) and ammonium hydroxide (0.04ml) were dissolved in ultra pure water (10ml). The mixed solution was transferred into a 20ml autoclave pressure vessel, followed by heating at 180°C for 3h and cooling to room temperature. The resulting d-GQDs were dissolved in ultra-pure water to form a transparent d-GQDs solution (60mgml−1). The acidity of the solution was adjusted to pH 7.0 by adding NaOH. The solution was filtered with 0.22μm filter membrane to remove larger product and by-products and then dialysed in a dialysis bag with a molecular weight cut-off 3kDa. Water was changed every 6h before precipitation occurred in the bag. The collected solution in the bag was dried by freeze-drying to obtain a solid sample of d-GQDs. Two control samples, butylamine-functionalized graphene quantum dots (B-GQDs) and octylamine-functionalized graphene quantum dots (O-GQDs), were also prepared by using the same procedure by using butylamine and octylamine instead of dodecylamine.

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and energy dispersive X-ray spectrum (EDX) analysis were performed using a HITACHI S4800 field emission scanning electron microscope. The SEM sample was prepared by dropping diluted ethanol dispersion of the products on a copper sheet attached to an aluminium sample holder, and the solvent was completely evaporated at room temperature. Transmission electron microscope (TEM) analysis was carried out on a JEOL 2010 (HR) transmission electron microscope at 200keV. The sample was prepared by dispersing a small amount of powder-like product into absolute ethanol. Then, one drop of the above suspension was dropped on the surface of 300 mesh copper and was dried under an infra-red lamp to volatilize the ethanol. X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern was obtained on a X-ray D8 Advance instrument operated at 40kV and 20mA using Cu Kα radiation source with λ=0.15406nm. Infrared spectrum (IR) was recorded on a Nicolet FT-IR 6700 spectrometer and Raman measurement was carried out using an InVia laser micro-Raman spectrometer. Fluorescence spectrum was recorded on a Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer (Varian, America). Optical microscopy image was recorded using a VHX-1000C Shooting optical microscope (Kean Co. Ltd, Hongkong). Zeta potential measurement was carried out in a ZETASIZER2000 Zeta Potential Analyser. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) image was obtained on a Multimode 8 force microscope (Bruker, Germany). The surface tension was determined on an OCA15EC video optical contact angle measurement instrument (Dataphysics, Germany). The fluorescence optical microscopy images of Pickering emulsion were recorded using an Eclipse 80i Fluorescence Microscope (Nikon, Japan).

Aqueous solution of d-GQDs (20mgml−1, 10ml) and styrene (10ml) were added into a centrifuge tube (50ml), followed by stirring for 10s at 10,000rpm on a HN-30K handheld homogenizer (Shanghai HANUO Instrument Co. Ltd, China) to obtain styrene in water emulsions.

The organic phase (1.5ml styrene and 7mg AIBN) was mixed with an aqueous d-GQDs solution (20mgml−1, 10ml) in a centrifuge tube and then stirred for 10s at 10,000rpm on the homogenizer. The tube was flushed with N2 during the reaction and the polymerization was conducted at 80°C for 8h. The collected precipitate was washed 3 times with ultrapure water and dried at 80°C to obtain d-GQDs-coated polystyrene spheres (d-GQDs/PS). The degree of monomer conversion was determined by gravimetry.

The surface tension of aqueous surfactant solution was measured using du Nouy ring method at 25±0.1°C. Interfacial tension (IFT) between aqueous solution and crude oil was measured using a Texas 500 Model Tensiometer at 45±0.1°C.

The GQDs prepared by using citric acid as the only carbon source contain rich hydrophilic groups at the edges of the graphene sheets [38,41–43]. Owing to its strong hydrophilicity and weak hydrophobicity, regular GQDs can be completely wetted by water. This characteristic causes GQDs to remain in a single water phase and a stable emulsion fails to form. To improve the amphiphilicity, amphiphilic GQDs, i.e., dodecylamine-functionalized graphene quantum dots (d-GQDs), were synthesis by thermal pyrolysis of citric acid and primary amine in an ammonium hydroxide solution (see Fig. 1 ). Here, citric acid is used to create hexagonal sp2-bonded carbon networks via the condensation between citric acid molecules and subsequent carbonization for forming graphene sheets. Primary amine is used to introduce alkyl chains into the edges of graphene sheets via the condensation between citric acid and amine molecules for enhancing the hydrophobicity. The ammonium hydroxide solution is used to increase the water-solubility of intermediates and final product to ensure the smooth progress of the synthesis. Although the reaction between carboxyl groups in citric acid and amino in amine groups can form stable chemical bonds due to their high reactive activities, the compounds formed by the condensation between citric acid and amine offer a much lower water-solubility compared to the compounds formed by the condensation between citric acid molecules. This investigation shows that precipitation soon appears in the system after the reaction begin. This action limits the growth of graphene sheets and even leads to the failure of the synthesis of d-GQDs. However, the use of a base medium increases the dissociation degree of the oxygen containing groups, thus leading to improved water-solubility. This is beneficial for accelerating the formation of graphene sheets.

To research the effect of amine content on the formation of graphene sheets, a series of d-GQDs products were prepared by changing the molar ratios of amine and citric acid (A/C). Their fluorescence spectra were measured and the results are shown in Fig. 2 . All fluorescence spectra display a similar shape and maximum fluorescence emission peak, indicating a similar size and size distribution of graphene sheets. The result verifies that the change in the A/C value in a certain range does not cause a significant change in the size and size distribution of graphene sheets. However, the fluorescence intensity seriously depends on the A/C value. The fluorescence intensity of d-GQDs increases with the increase of amine content when the ratio is less than 0.04, and reaches the maximum when the ratio is approximately 0.04. This could mainly be attributed to the formation of more graphene sheets with the increase of A/C value. However, the fluorescence intensity is reduced with the increase of the A/C value when the ratio is more than 0.04, and is close to zero when the ratio exceeds 0.1. At the same time, an obvious precipitation process appears in the sample solution (see insert in Fig. 2). This is because the reaction of citric acid with amine consumes more active groups in the citric acid molecule with the increase of amine content. This consumption reduces the ability to form graphene sheets by the thermal pyrolysis between citric acid molecules, thus leading to a rapid decrease in the size of graphene sheets and even failure of the synthesis of d-GQDs. To obtain the perfect structure of graphene sheet and introduce relatively more alkyl chains, an A/C value of 0.04 was employed for the synthesis.

The as-prepared d-GQDs were characterized by TEM, AFM, IR and XPS technologies. Fig. 3 presents the TEM image, particle size distribution and AFM image of d-GQDs. The d-GQDs show an approximately two-dimensional sheet shape with a low degree of agglomeration. The particle size distribution histogram is obtained by using an image processing software. The size of graphene sheets in the d-GQDs are distributed in a narrow range of 3–9nm with an average particle size of 5.6nm. In addition, the AFM image of d-GQDs in Fig.3D indicates that their heights are less than 2.0nm, suggesting that there are one to 5 layers of graphene oxide-like structures [44]. The above results also confirm that we obtained graphene quantum dots via a simple thermal pyrolysis of citric acid and amine.

Fig. 4 shows the IR and XPS spectra of d-GQDs. The band between 3300cm−1 and 3600cm−1 is the symmetric stretching vibration IR absorption of NH2 and OH. The peak at 1670cm−1 can be assigned to the CO stretch vibration. The peak at approximately 1381cm−1 could be attributed to CH stretching vibration, suggesting the presence of carbonyl and CH groups. The band at 3292cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibrations of COOH, NH2 and OH groups. Compared to citric acid, the d-GQDs show a simpler IR spectrum, and many weak IR absorption peaks in the IR spectrum of citric acid do not appear. This is because most of the active groups in citric acid were decomposed or consumed via polymerization and carbonization between small molecules during the formation of d-GQD. However, the characteristic IR absorption peaks of main functional groups in citric acid can be found in the IR spectrum of d-GQDs, including OH and COOH. In the C1s XPS spectrum there are three peaks. The peaks located at 284.6, 285.5 and 288.6eV can be assigned to CC, COH and CN species, respectively. There are three peaks at 398.4, 399.8 and 401.1eV in the N1s XPS spectrum, respectively. The peak at 398.4eV can be assigned to pyridinic N, the peak at 399.8eV can be assigned to amide, amine or pyrrolic N, and the peak at 401.1eV corresponds to graphitic N. There are two peaks at 531.8 and 533.1eV in the O1s XPS spectrum, corresponding to OC and OC. The results of IR and XPS analysis demonstrates that the d-GQDs contains NH2, COOH and OH, N CO groups.

The appropriate amphiphilic structure of d-GQDs may lead to high surface/interfacial activity. Fig.5 A presents the γ-log C curve of water in the presence of different concentrations of d-GQDs. It can be seen that the surface tension rapidly decreases with the increase of d-GQDs concentration when the d-GQDs concentration is lower than 0.0016gml−1 and then the trend becomes slow. The surface tension is almost unchanged when the concentration of d-GQDs exceeds 0.013gml−1. The minimum surface tension reaches 30.8mNm−1, indicating excellent surface activity. Fig.5B shows the interfacial tensions of paraffin oil/water in the presence of different concentrations of d-GQDs. The interfacial tension is rapidly reduced with the increase of d-GQDs concentration when the d-GQDs concentration is lower than 0.026gml−1. The tension slowly decrease with the concentration of d-GQDs when it is higher than 0.16gml−1. The minimum interfacial tension is 0.0182mNm−1, indicating high interfacial activity.

To investigate the effect of the number of carbon atoms in the alkyl chain on the surface activity, two types of GQDs with different alkyl chains were made and their surface tensions were measured. Fig. 6 shows that the surface tension of the d-GQDs aqueous solution is much lower than that of B-GQDs and O-GQDs, indicating much better surface activity. The main difference between the three types of graphene quantum dots is the number of carbon atoms in the alkyl chain. Thus the result demonstrates that the number of carbon atoms in the alkyl chain is critical factor affecting the surface activity of d-GQDs. The longer the alkyl chain, the better its surface activity. The above law is consistent with classical surface chemistry theory. Fig.6B presents the surface tensions of solid particle surfactants that have been often used for stabilizing Pickering emulsion in the literature and sodium dodecylbenzenesulfonate (SDBS), which is classical organic molecular surfactant. The surface tension of GQD prepared by thermal hydrolysis of citric acid is approximately 71.8mNm−1, which is close to that of pure water (72.2mNm−1). The result verifies that the GQDs have poor surface activity. This is attributed to its inappropriate amphiphilicity. Owing to the too strong hydrophilicity and too weak hydrophobicity, the graphene sheets can be completely wetted by water. These remain in a single water phase and a stable emulsion fails to form. Compared with the GQDs, graphene oxide (GO), graphene (e) and Cu2O nanoparticles offer lower water-solubility. These can reduce the surface tension of water to between 50 and 60mNm−1, indicating a poor surface activity. This is mainly attributed to their strong hydrophobicity and weak hydrophilicity. In addition, large 2D graphene sheets in graphene and GO easily aggregated on the fluid interface. This increases the interfacial layer rigidity, thus leading to a further reduction of the surface activity. From Fig.6B, we find that the surface tension of SDBS is 32.4mNm−1. This is slightly higher that of the d-GQDs aqueous solution, verifying that the surface activity of d-GQDs is close to that of a classical organic molecular surfactant. The high surface activity may greatly improve the stability of Pickering emulsions.

The edge of graphene sheets in the d-GQDs show a variety of hydrophilic groups. These hydrophilic groups may be protonated at a relatively high acidity and dissociated at a relatively low acidity, which has been confirmed by zeta potential measurement in Fig. s1. Both the dissociation and protonation in water may influence the amphiphilicity of d-GQDs, resulting in a change in the surface and interfacial activity. To examine the effect of medium acidity on the surface activity, the surface tension of 0.02gml−1 of d-GQDs aqueous solution at different pH was measured and the result is listed in Fig. 7 . The surface tensions at different pH show an obvious difference, verifying that the pH of the medium can influence the surface activity of d-GQDs. The surface tension is between 50 and 40mNm−1 when the pH is less than 4, indicating a relatively low surface activity. The surface tension is approximately 35 mN m−1 when the pH is between 4 and 9, indicating excellent surface activity. The surface tension reaches its minimum value at pH 7. However, the surface tension abruptly increases when the pH is more than 9, indicating a relatively low surface activity. We suggest that the behaviour at different pH is mainly attributed to the protonation of amino groups at a relatively low pH and the dissociation of carboxyl groups at a relatively high pH value. Both the protonation and dissociation lead to an increase of the d-GQDs polarity. The enhanced water-solubility destroys the balance of amphiphilicity in the neutral d-GQDs and results in the decrease of surface activity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characteristic of the Pickering emulsions stabilized by d-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The d-GQDs were employed as a solid particle surfactant for stabilizing styrene-in-water emulsions. Optical microscopy and fluorescent microscopy images of the Pickering emulsions stabilized by d-GQDs are shown in Fig. s2. It can be seen from Fig. s2A that all oil droplets in the Pickering emulsions are regular spheres with a relatively narrow size distribution. Owing to its high hydrophilicity, the d-GQDs tend to stabilize oil-in-water emulsions. The graphene sheets of d-GQDs are assembled at the styrene/water interface, because of the reduced total interfacial energy that occurs when a part of the liquid-liquid interface is replaced with a liquid-particle interface. The styrene is covered by small graphene sheets and lies at the centre of the droplets. This is also confirmed by the result of fluorescent microscopy analysis in Fig. s2B. To evaluate the effect of surfactants on the emulsion stability, four types of the emulsions stabilized by d-GQDs, GO, graphene and SDBS were made by using the same procedure. The formed emulsions were treated by centrifugal separation at 1000rpm. Optical photographs of the emulsions at different times are shown in Figs. s3 and s4. For the emulsions with d-GQDs no phase separation can be observed within 30min, indicating excellent stability. For the emulsions with GO and grapheme, an obvious phase separation begins to occur after 10s. The process is completed within 0.5min, indicating poor emulsion stability. The emulsions with SDBS can be stabilized for at least 25min, indicating high emulsion stability. The above results verify that the emulsions with d-GQDs exhlbit excellent stability, which is much better than the emulsions with GO and graphene and is close to the emulsions with SDBS.

To study the response of the Pickering emulsions to the acidity of the medium, styrene-in-water emulsions were made by using the same procedure described in Section 2.4. The Pickering emulsions are a homogeneous system and no phase separation can be observed, indicating one “on” state. After that, HCI or NaOH aqueous solution (6M, 10μl) was added into the above emulsions and subsequently stirred for 10s at 10,000rpm on the homogenizer. The result shows that an obvious phase separation soon occurs after the stirring ends, and the system is rapidly divided into separate styrene phase and water phase, indicating one “off” state. The result indicates that the Pickering emulsions stabilized by d-GQD show a sensitive response to the acidity of the medium. Furthermore, the repeatability of “on” and “off” behaviours was tested by alternately adding HCI aqueous solution (6M, 10μl) and NaOH aqueous solution (6M, 10μl). The above processes were repeated eight times and the optical photographs of the system for each “on” and “off” are shown in Fig. 8 . The result shows that the emulsion can be well recovered by the addition of NaOH after each “off” state with HCI, or the addition of HCI after each “on” state with NaOH, indicating excellent pH-switching behaviour. For the application of solid particles as the surfactant in emulsion polymerization, the pH-switching behaviour may play an important role in product separation and surfactant reuse.

To achieve the emulsion polymerization of styrene, styrene containing AIBN was mixed with d-GQDs solution to form stable Pickering emulsions. AIBN was used as an initiator for triggering the emulsion polymerization of styrene, leading to the formation of polystyrene spheres (PS). The reaction was conducted with stirring at 80°C for 8h. The obtained PS particles were characterized by SEM, EDS and fluorescent microscopic technologies. Fig.9 A shows that the PS particles are regular spheres with an average particle size of 204nm. The particle size distribution is in the range from 198nm to 210nm, indicating excellent uniformity. The uniformity is much better than that of other methods for the preparation of PS and other polymerized spheres with a surfactant as the soft template [38]. Fig.9B shows the total EDS element image. The EDS analysis reveals that the PS sample consists of carbon, nitrogen and oxygen elements. Among these elements, the carbon element could be attributed to d-GQDs and PS. The nitrogen and oxygen elements are attributed to d-GQDs, indicating the existence of d-GQDs. In addition, the EDS images in Fig.9C and D indicate that the nitrogen and oxygen elements have excellent dispersion. The high dispersion of nitrogen and oxygen elements demonstrates that the d-GQDs were well coated on the surface of PS spheres during synthesis of PS. Fig. s5 presents the fluorescent microscopic image of the PS sample. As PS itself is not a photoactive material, it is impossible to produce a visible fluorescence under UV excitation. Thus, the strong fluorescence emission is only attributed to d-GQDs. This result again verifies that d-GQDs exist on the surfaces of PS spheres.

To understand the mechanism for the emulsion polymerization of styrene, the effect of d-GQDs concentration on emulsion polymerization of styrene was investigated. Fig. 10 presents the SEM images of d-GQDs/PS products prepared with different concentrations of d-GQDs. All of the products display a smooth appearance, and no rigid interfacial layer can be observed on the surface of d-GQDs/PS. The reason is because d-GQDs contain rich hydrophilic groups on the edge of graphene sheets, and their dissociation generates negatively chargedgraphene sheets. The electrostatic repulsion between different graphene sheets prevents the aggregation of small graphene sheets on the fluid interface and efficiently avoids the formation of a rigid interfacial layer. Fig. 10 also shows that the d-GQDs/PS product exhibits a uniform morphology with a narrow diameter distribution when the concentration of A-GQDs is lower than 60mgml−1. The particle size of d-GQDs/PS decreases with the increase of d-GQDs concentration. Highly hydrophilic graphene sheets are dispersed in water, and the polymerization of styrene initiated by AIBN first takes place in the oil phase. The PS oligomers precipitate from the monomer phase as the chains grow due to their poor solubility. Considering the existence of hydrophobic alkyl chains at the edges of graphene sheets, this type of interaction is most likely hydrophobic force between the alkyl chain and the PS oligomers. The hydrophilicity of graphene sheets in the d-GQDs is reduced dominantly after the attachment of hydrophobic PS chains. The improvement in hydrophobicity induces the aggregation of PS chains to produce nuclei that can be quickly swollen by the monomer. The nascent nuclei grow in size with further polymerization. Because the growing interfacial area is stabilized by d-GQDs remaining in the aqueous phase [29], the formed interface becomes more stable and the number of nuclei rapidly increases with the increase of d-GQDs concentration in the aqueous phase. As a result, the formation of more d-GQDs/PS shows a reduced particle size. However, micron-sized d-GQDs/PS appears in the product when the d-GQDs concentration reaches 60mgml−1, which could be attributed to droplet nucleation. At d-GQDs concentration of more than 60mgml−1, there is a sufficient amount of surfactants to provide enough stabilization for the oil/water interface, resulting in enhanced possibility of droplet nucleation [14].

This study demonstrated a facile synthesis of dodecylamine-functionalized graphene quantum dots. Owing to the appropriate amphiphilic structure, the graphene quantum dots exhibited much better surface activity compared to graphene [30,38,39], regular graphene oxide [4,45], small graphene oxide [29–34] and common graphene quantum dots [36]. The surface activity was much better than that of previously reported solid particle surfactants for stabilizing Pickering emulsions [7–16] and was close to that of classical organic surfactants. The stabilized styrene-in-water emulsions with the graphene quantum dots showed an excellent stability and pH-switching behaviour. 2,2′-Azobis(2-methylpropionitrile) was introduced into the Pickering emulsions to trigger the emulsion polymerization of styrene. The as-prepared polystyrene spheres displayed a uniform morphology with a narrow diameter distribution. The fluorescent d-GQDs well coated their surfaces. This study provides a prominent approach for the fabrication of amphiphilic GQDs and GQDs-based functional materials, which have a wide range of potential applications in emulsion polymerization, sensor, catalyst, and energy storage.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21576115) and the Prospective Joint Research Project: Cooperative Innovation Fund (No. BY2015019-26).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2017.06.091.
